frameworks.name: object-centric representation learning
frameworks.one_line: Scenes are decomposed into discrete object slots, each a disentangled latent.

frameworks.name: slot attention
frameworks.one_line: Iterative attention-based slot binding extended with linguistic grounding.

frameworks.name: language-grounded concept learning
frameworks.one_line: Word-to-slot associations bias unsupervised object discovery toward nameable concepts.

frameworks.name: MONet (mixture-of-experts scene decomposition)
frameworks.one_line: Generative slot-wise scene decomposition baseline augmented by language signal.

core_question: Can language input improve the quality of unsupervised object-centric scene representations and the concepts they carry?
key_assumptions: Scenes are compositionally decomposable into a fixed set of object slots
Words denoting categories, properties, and spatial relations can be aligned to slot-level latents
Reconstruction-only unsupervised object discovery underfits semantically meaningful concepts
manipulations: Presence vs absence of paired language input during representation learning
Choice of underlying language-agnostic object discovery backbone (MONet, Slot Attention)
Type of linguistic concept supplied (categories, properties, spatial relations)
key_findings: LORL consistently improves unsupervised object discovery performance on two datasets
Concepts induced via language transfer benefit downstream referring expression comprehension
Language-mediated training is compatible with multiple language-agnostic discovery algorithms
interpretations: Language acts as a weak supervisory signal that disentangles and labels object-centric latents
Concept grounding and object discovery are mutually reinforcing rather than independent
connecting_signals: concept learning from vision and language
